speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. And we're in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, you said that you went back to Amsterdam and you needed papers to-- to

speaker: interviewee
text: To travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: To travel and to live with--

speaker: interviewee
text: And to stay with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know-- how did you know that you needed all this official--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it difficult to get?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There was no problem. My doctor gave me straightaway a certificate. And then on the 6th of August, '42, there was a pickup from the whole street where my parents lived. And there were two main roads. And in between street, that's where my parents lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of where your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 6 [INAUDIBLE]. And they lived there in a flat. And next door lived my mother's sister with her husband and two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Els [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Brande, B-R-A-N-D-E-R. Brande. Anyway, then [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] my father fled to the roof and they didn't find him. And we had a man in our place who was living with a non-Jewish woman for more than 25 years. But he couldn't do that anymore, so he stayed with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Louis Suster. And then when they collected everybody, then I had to go down too. So we were staying on the street, waiting 'til the whole street was picked up. And I was very hungry, because I was in expecting. And I said to my mother, I'm so hungry. I would like to eat something. And my mother, who was scared for everything, said, oh, go to that policeman, a Dutch policeman, and ask them if you can get something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had nothing to say, so he called a German. And he said [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I could prove that I was pregnant. And luckily, I had that letter in my pocket, so I showed that. And then he let me go. But you didn't know what you were doing, so I went upstairs, made a big packet with bread rolls and whatever that was, and went downstairs to bring it to my mother and my auntie and my uncle and the other man. And when I came downstairs, they were gone. So somebody walked on the street. I said, where are they gone? Oh, they're around the corner in the big open trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went around the corner and wanted to hand over the-- the food. And the German came and got me in my neck and said, meet train, meet. You have to come. And I screamed my head off. I don't have to go. And I'm pregnant and let me go. And anyway, he let me go. So I went back to the flat. And I didn't come out any more, of course, until it got dark. And then I went upstairs and got my father from the roof. And then I went-- he had arranged already an hiding place. And that-- where the woman was Jewish, but he was an Italian man. And we went there. And there I stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- who were they? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bolognese. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. And we had made [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- what do you call that, under the roof? How you call that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The ceiling. The attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The attic. In the attic, we had made a false wall, so that in case the German came, we could hide behind that. And I slept there with the maid. They had a maid, a live-in maid. They were well-to-do people. And I slept there with that girl. And then in the daytime, I went down in the lounge with my father, and my father's second wife. He had a girlfriend then. And that was a big hassle. It was a big thing for me, because my mother was just taken away and then I was confronted with her. I knew all about it, but I never met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a terrible time, big fights every day. And then I got very big, big for the baby, and we had to find a doctor. And then somebody recommended a doctor, which was the-- the director of a Catholic hospital in Amsterdam. And we approached him and he said he was willing to take me in hospital-- Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --when the time was there. No, I don't. I only know where he lived, but that's all. And then we had to go regular for checkups. And then in the dark, I went with my father. I was that big, enormous. And when I went a couple of times, then I said to him, listen, doctor. I don't want my son to be registered. And he said, that is a bit of a problem. I have to register--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean your baby? You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the baby. And I said I don't want that, because as soon as you register a baby, they know it's a Jewish child. And he said, I can't do that. That is against the law and all that business. Anyway, just a short while before the baby was going to be born, he said he couldn't take me to hospital. It was too risky, because there were so many people working there and maybe one would find out that I was Jewish. So he couldn't take the risk, because then he would go to camp too. So I had to find somebody. But I didn't want to stay with my father. And then I took a private nurse. I found somebody who had an-- an home what was originally from the welfare. And there were 20 old people and children from parents who worked in Germany or split-up parents and neglected children, about 30 kids. Anyway, I talked in one of those nurses to stay with me. And then I went back to my own flat. And we decided that in case the German came, I would say I'm due any minute. I was so enormous, so I could easily say that. But I didn't know where to put the baby. Nobody wanted the baby. I didn't know what to do. And that man what was caught the same time as my mom [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] suggested to me to leave it at her doorstep. But I said no. I don't take the risk, because if somebody find out-- she brought a lot of kids into hiding. And I said no, I can't do that, because that is too risky. And then he was born on a Sunday. And on the Wednesday, I had to go in in that home and do-- induce the birth. So on Sunday, he was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what the date was when he was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24th of January '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where-- where did you give birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that home. On the [INAUDIBLE]. And then the sixth day, somebody from the underground movement came-- so he was six days old-- drove me back to the place where I was hiding and took the baby with them. I didn't know where or what or who, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that organized with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a friend of my father worked in underground movement. And on the day he was born, he said, I found a spot for him. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Isdein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, Selien, when you went to hiding with the Bolognese family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then with the doctor, was all that for payment or how was that organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bolognese [INAUDIBLE], yes, we had to pay, but it was not so much. But that woman, that Jewish woman had cinemas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Bolognese?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She inherited from her father in a little town in [INAUDIBLE]. And she was involved in the underground movement. And one day she came home and she said, you can't stay here, because somebody is caught and there's a possibility that they come here. It's too risky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to go out straight away. And I went to that lady who wanted to bring my baby-- pick my baby up. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andre Lebon. She was Belgian. And I stayed with her couple of nights. And then my father went somewhere else with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just--

speaker: interviewee
text: And another couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you, Selien, what happened to your husband during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we were caught in [INAUDIBLE], my father through his second wife had connections. That's how I came free. It was the only good thing about her. [LAUGHS] And my husband, they didn't let him go. He stayed there. And while he was there, I arranged to bribe a warden so that he could get food. And I sewed in shoulder pads money to bribe the guy to get food in under the pretense that he need different clothes. And that went on for a while, until he was caught. And he had a guy with him in his cell. And then they were sent to Mauthausen and that-- they killed him there straightaway. And he was 22 years old. That was December '42. And it was an exception in those days if they notified you. But they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they notify you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the baby was not born then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they notify you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A letter. I still have a copy here. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, then-- where was I?

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to leave the Bolognese house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then I went to Hausen. And that was a retired police detective.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the underground movement. My father was there, so I went there too. And-- but he was a terrible man. And we had to pay a lot of money to stay there. But when the baby was born, we had to pay him a lot of money too every day. And my father had to bring me meat, otherwise I wouldn't have hardly anything to eat, because that home belonged to two nurses, and one of them was a very mad woman. And she got-- for all those kids she got coupons for food. But she sold the coupons. And she had a child. I don't know how she got that child, if she adopted that or whatever. It was not they easy in Haarlem to adopt a child. But anyway, she had a little girl. And that girl was all dressed up and good-looking and ate whatever there was. But she did it all from the coupons from the kids. Anyway, she was caught later on. But let me first continue with-- then when I had the baby, I left there. And then I want to Hausen. And then I ran away there, because I was very restless, of course. And then I went back there and asked if I could work for her as an assistant nurse. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can't remember. And then I worked there from 7 o'clock in the morning 'til 7 o'clock at night. Every morning for 30 kids i had to do the washing on the washing board, not in a machine, because there was no clothes. There was no money. There was nothing. And those kids came in really neglected, full is lice. And you had to cut the clothes off them and put them in special baths. And it was horrible. And on top of that, I had to pay 65 guilders a week, on top of my work. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There was another Jewish lady there, who worked in the kitchen. I don't know her name. I only know we called her Baker, [INAUDIBLE]. And near the entrance from that-- it was a very big house. Near the entrance from that house was a grill next to the front entrance. And there was a wooden floor. And we lifted that wooden floor up about that big to make an hiding place. But when you went in there, you had to stand up and let yourself down and let yourself go on the knees to lay in there. You couldn't sit. And one day, the German came and we had to go in there, that woman and me. And then she said, I'm suffocated. I'm suffocated. And I was scared stiff, because they were standing on top of us. I could hear every word they were saying. They were looking for Jews, but they didn't fund us, luckily. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then those-- after about half-year, I had enough of it. And I was sleeping with an qualified nurse in the same room. And I said to her, I can't take it anymore. I'm leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you going? I said, I don't know. She said, I got a friend who is in the underground movement. And if you like, I'll contact him. And then maybe he can bring you over the border and you can go to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said OK. Cost 5,000 guilders. Well, I didn't have the money. I had jewelry, so I sold it. I had to pay 2 and 1/2 before I left, and 2 and 1/2 when I arrived in Switzerland. And I had my suitcase packed. I didn't tell anything to my father. But that Belgian lady always came-- I came to her once a week or so. And I told her, and she told my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the day I was going to leave, was a knock on the door. And there was the son from the policeman. And I said, what are you doing here? He said, you have to go with me back to Hausen. I said, no, I'm not going back. He said, you're not going over the border, because there's a chance when you come outside that they'll bring you to the German. So I had no choice. And I left that place and I went to Hausen. And we stayed there for quite a while and we paid a lot of money there. There were people who did it for nothing, but we've been not so lucky. Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and his wife and another couple. And-- and then after a while, I left there too. And then I went to Bussum. And that was the wife from our accountant. But they were split up and they had a big house and three kids and a brother-in-law was living there. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did the cleaning and everything. And then after a little while, there was no food anymore. And so we had to go to the country to get food. And I had false papers, so I went on my bike with her brother-in-law. And he-- he had one leg he couldn't bend. But he was also on a bike with one pedal. And we-- we left-- first she left. And she came back with food. And then I had to go, so I went. I was not scared at all in those days. I didn't look Jewish. I was blonde and I was half the size what I'm now, so didn't worry me at all. I really had guts in those days, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went. Pouring rain. You have no idea. And we had to go through the polders. And there was water on that side and water on that side. What-- what are polders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polders are pieces of land for the farmers. But there was both sides water and water from the-- from upstairs, more than enough. So I was soaking wet. And then we came in a small town, Harderwijk. And I approached people on the street if I could find any food there, because I was hungry. So they told me, you go there and there. There is a kitchen from the German. And you go and ask them for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did. And I went in. And I said, can you give me something to eat? And they talked together and they said yes. I said, but I've got a friend with me. So I called him in and we had most beautiful meal, because they had everything, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had to have accommodation. So I was told by somebody on the street, you go to the police, get a ticket, and you go down there is a hotel. And they take all those people who are looking for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did. Then by the police, I said to him, Bill, you go in. I'll wait outside, because I had only false papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went in. We got a ticket . And we went to the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was full with people, because hundreds of people were looking for food, you know? So when we came in, the owners said, sorry. I got only one room left. with a double bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, oh, no way. I'm not going to bed with him. He was a very good man, nice meeting, man, but not from me. So he said, well, that's all that's left. I said, oh, no. And then there was a woman with her husband. And she said, I want to help you. I said, oh, that's nice. You sleep with me and he can sleep with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought, it's terrific. So we took our clothes off. There was a big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] heater there, what they build up with-- with blocks of wood. And everybody put their wet clothes around it to get dry clothes for the next day. So we go to bed. And I was shivering. I was cold and went and-- and she came a bit closer to me. And I came a bit closer to her. I was glad I had somebody next to me. And then in the dark, she said to me-- because it was everywhere dark. She said to me, where are you from? I said, from Bussum. Oh, I'm from Hilversum. And Hilversum is where the radio stations are in Haarlem.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, oh, yes. Yes, my husband worked for the radio stations. I said, oh. And then I don't know how it developed and she said, yes, I used to work for Jews, but I hate them. And I thought, my god, you must know that you're laying next to one, but I didn't say a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next morning, I said to Bill what I had a-- an upheaval in the night. I said I was so upset, but I couldn't say a word. I couldn't talk myself in. So we went further. And then I was so tired. I couldn't take it anymore. So all of a sudden I said to him, I'm going to stop a truck from the German. So I did. And I said, can I have a lift? Yes. And it was a driver and a guy next to him in uniform. And they lifted the two bikes on the truck. And I was sitting in the front, next to the driver, because that other guy went in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they drove us to a little hotel, where all German there. And we could stay there overnight. And in the night, I had to go to the bathroom. And there were all-- every room had boots outside the doors. And I got mixed up and I walked in the wrong room, of course. And anyway, the next morning we had a beautiful breakfast. We had eggs, which was a luxury. Nobody had eggs. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went on and went to farmers to ask for food and-- anyway, we got food.

speaker: interviewer
text: But by the beginning of the tour, we went to a farmer and he said, I haven't got anything, but I got a big egg, an ostrich egg. And it was that big. And, you want it? I said yes. And I had a legal tin with me where I had a few slices of bread in when I left. And I put that in the tin.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when that women where I was staying we came home from the first tour, she brought in bacon and eggs and what have you. And she made a party for herself, but we didn't get a bit of it. So I said to Bill, when we come back, I'll have that egg and then we have a party.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I put that in my father's room. And she had to go somewhere, to Amsterdam or something. And I said to him, today is egg day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said listen, Selien, I'm thinking about it, but I can't do that to my sister-in-law. And I said, look, Bill, when she came back, she had a party. Now it's my party. He said no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was nervous wreck in those days, and I said, no? He said, no. I don't want it. I said, you don't want it? Then nobody has it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the toilet, took the egg, and smashed it in the toilet. I had to clean the whole bathroom, because it went to pieces, of course. But I couldn't care less. I didn't have the egg, so she didn't have the egg. Maybe not fair, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here, Selien.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's that end of Tape 2 of the interview with Selien Abram. It's the 12th of July, 1996. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
